name: italian minestrone soup
notes:
Italian grandma soup (a.k.a. minestrone). A clean italian soup. Soffrito (yellow onion, celery, carrot) + tomato (both fresh AND paste) as the base, white beans as the protein, then optional seasoning directions on top.

TOMATO IS NON-OPTIONAL — BOTH FORMS: every time I've left out either fresh tomatoes OR tomato paste, I've been disappointed. The soup ends up flat and unsatisfying. Fresh tomatoes give body and brightness; tomato paste gives concentrated depth and color. Together they make the base sing; alone neither is enough.

FIRE ROASTED TOMATOES CLASH: do NOT reach for fire-roasted canned tomatoes here. Their smoky char pulls the soup in a TexMex / sofrito direction that fights the clean italian register the dish is trying to hold. If you have fire-roasted tomatoes in the cupboard that you're trying to use up, this is not the recipe for that — make a chili or a mexican stew instead.

SEASONING DIRECTIONS — ALL OPTIONAL: previously I treated old bay + bay leaf, fennel, and porcini powder as three required flavoring directions. They're not required — the soup works on the tomato + soffrito + bean + olive oil + garlic + black pepper base alone. Treat these as optional add-ons for variety:
- Old bay + bay leaf: shifts the soup toward a more savory-american register.
- Fennel (powder or bulb): adds anise depth.
- Porcini mushroom powder: adds earthy umami.
Pick one or none. Layering more than one is busy.

THINK OF IT AS BETWEEN A LATHERA AND A BEAN SOUP: expect a shallow broth, not a rich gravy. The dish sits between a greek lathera (vegetable + olive oil braise) and an italian bean soup. The character comes from olive oil + beans + garlic + black pepper + the tomato base. Don't try to push it into a stew-rich body with stock and MSG additions — that's a different (and worse) dish.

STOCK — ONLY IF YOU'RE ADDING PASTA: stock is normally an antipattern in this soup (the soffrito + tomato builds enough body), but there's one exception: if you want to add small pasta, you need a bit of stock to give the pasta something to cook in. The tomato-only broth doesn't have enough liquid volume to handle pasta absorption without going gummy.

SMALL PASTA ONLY (MACARONI, ORZO, DITALINI): the pasta has to actually be small. The vegetable broth created by the tomatoes can't support large pasta — egg noodles, full-length spaghetti, or larger shapes will sit on top and never integrate. Macaroni, orzo, ditalini, or other small pasta cuts work. Add LESS pasta than you think you need: this is a bean soup with pasta, not a pasta soup with beans. Focus the volume on green beans, white beans, and carrot greens; the pasta is supplemental.

PARMESAN RIND: an italian-grandma move. Drop a parmesan rind into the broth as it simmers. The rind gives body and a faint cheesy depth that takes the dish from generic bean soup to something more interesting. Pull the rind out before serving (or leave it in as a small treat for whoever finds it).

GREEN BEANS OVER ZUCCHINI: zucchini technically works in this soup but reads as a downgrade from green beans. Green beans give discrete textural chew and structural integrity; zucchini turns soft and merges into the broth. If you're choosing between the two, pick green beans. Seeded zucchini (the kind you've already deseeded to avoid bitter water) is acceptable but still inferior to green beans here.

FENNEL BULB IS OK BUT HAS BETTER USES: fennel bulb technically works in this soup as part of the fennel flavoring direction, but fennel bulb usually has better things to be doing on your plate — in a salad, in a slaw, in apple-fennel-salad. The soup absorbs the bulb's character without showcasing it; you lose the textural and structural appeal of the bulb itself. Use fennel powder for the flavoring direction; save the bulb for a dish that lets it shine.

DICING THE SOFFRITO — FINELY DICED, NOT PROCESSED: the yellow onion, celery, and carrot should be finely diced but kept slightly chunky — not pulverized in a food processor. The soup wants a bit of textural chew from the soffrito; processing it to a paste eliminates that chew and pulls the dish toward a smooth blended soup (which is a different recipe). Default cutting-board instincts are probably right — a normal small dice gets the size you want. This is unusual for the cookbook (other dishes like chile colorado and adobo black bean stew specifically want food-processor texture for their bases); italian grandma soup is the opposite case.

EARTHY NEUTRAL GREENS OVER FINE HERBS: this soup wants flavorless leafy greens (spinach, mixed greens, carrot greens) rather than fine herbs (parsley, tarragon). The soffrito-and-tomato base is delicate, and fine herbs steamroll it — they fight whichever flavoring direction you've chosen (if any) and muddle the dish. Neutral earthy greens, by contrast, add volume, bitterness, nutrition, and color without competing with the established flavors. Reach for the greens drawer, not the herb drawer. Robust herbs (rosemary, thyme, oregano) are OK as secondary additions for variety, but unnecessary — the tomato + olive oil + garlic + black pepper base already carries the dish.

GLUTAMATE INJECTIONS ARE ANTIPATTERNS: nutritional yeast, soy sauce, dark miso, bouillon cubes, etc. distract from the lathera-style restraint the dish wants. Porcini powder is the one exception (it's one of the optional flavoring directions). When the soup tastes flat, the move is more olive oil, more garlic, more black pepper, more time — not MSG additions.

BEANS: white beans are superior to lentils, but both work. Both are better than chickpeas, which are too harsh and break the dish.

POTATOES: optional structural addition. Soaks up the tomato base, gives the soup more chew.

CARROT GREENS: a real ingredient here, not a garnish. Adds volume, color, and a vegetal note that supports the tomato + soffrito base.
